coredata.dc:title: Toward efficient broad-spectrum UV absorption of carbon dots: Facile preparation, performance characterization and its application as UV absorbers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Jiemin
Ye, Weihao
Chen, Congcong
Xu, Zhiqiang
Hu, Chaofan
Zhuang, Jianle
Dong, Hanwu
Lei, Bingfu
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: UV absorbers are sustainable substances that inhibit the ultraviolet (UV) radiative degradation of polymers. In previous reports, the as-prepared carbon dots (CDs) possess only an absorption band at UVA (320–400 nm). To achieve broad-spectrum UV absorption (200–400 nm), increasing the concentration of the CDs solution becomes common. However, a high concentration solution seriously affects the light transmittance due to its heavy yellow color. Furthermore, the promising organic UV absorbers cannot achieve broad-spectrum UV absorption. In this work, we initially synthesized three-component water-soluble carbon dots (OP–CDs), which delivers an excellent UV absorption (98 % UVA and 100 % UVB at the concentration of 1.7 × 10−2 mg/mL; 99 % transmittance in visible light). Moreover, OP–CDs exhibit outstanding stability at high temperatures and different pH. With the corporation of OP–CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a high-performance UV-shielding film was formed, leading to enhanced broad-spectrum UV shielding performance and improved mechanical properties, resistance of photocatalytic activity and anti-UV aging ability compared with pure PVA film. Notably, the PVA film remains high transparency with the addition of OP–CDs. The study suggests that the corporation of OP–CDs and polymers is effective in anti-UV degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UV absorbers
Broad-spectrum UV absorption
Stability
Mechanical properties
Photocatalytic activity
Anti-UV aging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic route of OP–CDs and their application as the UV absorber for PVA films.
(a) The TEM image, (b) the HR-TEM image, (c) the Particle size histogram, (d) the XRD pattern, (e) the FT-IR spectrum, (f) the XPS survey, (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s and (i) O 1 s XPS spectra of OP-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra of OP–CDs (the inset: photographs of OP–CDs under daylight and UV light). (b) PL spectra of OP–CDs solution, (c) and (d) Effect of pH on UV–vis absorption spectrum of OP–CDs. (e) and (f) Transmittance spectra of OP–CDs under exposure to the influence of different times at 60 ℃ and the outdoor at different times (the inset: photographs of OP–CDs under daylight and UV light).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of OP–CDs@PVA films prepared using different OP–CDs contents. (b) The corresponding transmittance spectra of OP–CDs@PVA films, (c) The UV-shielding ability of OP–CDs@PVA under sunlight. (d) The UV-shielding ability of OP–CDs@PVA after 120 h exposed to sunlight.
(a) Photographs of OP–CDs@PVA films with different concentrations of OP–CDs (0.1–2.7 mg). (b) Photograph of OP-CDs@PVA films blocking UV light by UV lamp of Rhodamine B.

(a) PL emission spectra of OP–CDs@PVA films, (b) the transmittance intensity of OP–CDs@PVA-6 film for 120 h under UV irradiation (30 W). (c) The changes UV–vis intensity of fluorescein sodium salt after different times of UV irradiation (the inset: photograph of fluorescein sodium salt after 12 h UV radiation). (d) The stress–strain curves of the PVA and OP–CDs@PVA films.

head.ce:title: Toward efficient broad-spectrum UV absorption of carbon dots: Facile preparation, performance characterization and its application as UV absorbers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: UV absorbers are sustainable substances that inhibit the ultraviolet (UV) radiative degradation of polymers. In previous reports, the as-prepared carbon dots (CDs) possess only an absorption band at UVA (320–400 nm). To achieve broad-spectrum UV absorption (200–400 nm), increasing the concentration of the CDs solution becomes common. However, a high concentration solution seriously affects the light transmittance due to its heavy yellow color. Furthermore, the promising organic UV absorbers cannot achieve broad-spectrum UV absorption. In this work, we initially synthesized three-component water-soluble carbon dots (OP–CDs), which delivers an excellent UV absorption (98 % UVA and 100 % UVB at the concentration of 1.7 × 10−2 mg/mL; 99 % transmittance in visible light). Moreover, OP–CDs exhibit outstanding stability at high temperatures and different pH. With the corporation of OP–CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a high-performance UV-shielding film was formed, leading to enhanced broad-spectrum UV shielding performance and improved mechanical properties, resistance of photocatalytic activity and anti-UV aging ability compared with pure PVA film. Notably, the PVA film remains high transparency with the addition of OP–CDs. The study suggests that the corporation of OP–CDs and polymers is effective in anti-UV degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation is generally divided into three band regions: UVA (320–400 nm), UVB (320–280 nm) and UVC (280–100 nm) [1,2]. Overexposure to UV radiation significantly impacts the human body, tissue, and physiology through photochemical reactions [3]. For example, the skin can rapidly produce melanin under UVA, resulting in the darkening of the skin. In severe cases, UVA will penetrate the dermis layer of the skin and induce photoaging and discoloration of the skin. UVB region is one of the more energetic wavelengths and can bring about severe sunburns such as erythema and blisters [4]. UVC region is absorbed mainly by the ozone layer and therefore is less concerned from a health perspective. It was worth noting that UVA and UVB regions are the most harmful to human activities and the ecological environment, especially the polymer films widely used in various industries. Long-term UV radiation (200–400 nm) will excite the polymer materials to generate free radicals and accelerate the destruction of the polymer chain, resulting in obvious deformation and yellowing on the surface of the material. [5,6] The mechanical properties are also severely reduced, which ultimately shortens the service life of the film [7]. To improve the UV resistance of polymer materials, Li et al. [8,9] modified polyimide (PI) by doping with silica zeolite nanoparticles or true squid melanin to prepare hybrid films. Wang et al. [10] investigated the ability of bio-macromolecular true melanin compounded with polyvinyl alcohol PVA to block most UV rays below 300 nm. Liu et al. [11] prepared highly efficient microwave absorbing materials by wrapping multi-walled carbon nanotubes around metal nanoparticles on collagen fiber membranes. Titanium dioxide nanoparticles films with good UV-shielding properties were prepared using cellulose triacetate (CTA) and sol–gel method by Gomez-Hermosa-de-Mendoza et al. [12]. Although polymer materials have made progressive achievements in the development of UV resistance, having excellent transparency and UV resistance still needs further improvement.

UV absorbers are light stabilizers that prevent polymer degradation from sunlight or other artificial UV light and are classified as inorganic absorbers and organic absorbers. Inorganic absorbers are composed of various metal oxides and the wavelength range is below 400 nm, such as TiO2, ZnO, CeO2 and CuO, etc. [13–15]. They have scattering and reflection properties, so they have strong UV resistance. However, the most inorganic absorbers have photocatalytic properties, generating reactive oxygen species to accelerate the degradation of polymer film under long-term UV radiation [16,17]. Once the content increases, it is easy to agglomerate in the polymer film, affecting the optical transparency and mechanical properties [18,19]. Organic absorbers include benzophenone, benzotriazole, salicylic acid and triazine absorbers [20]. They are the most widely used and diverse UV absorbers, non-negligible toxicity, narrow UV-shielding range and non-water solubility are often limiting their applications [21]. Many researchers have made efforts to develop broad-spectrum UV absorbers.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery and catalysis due to excellent optical properties, simple preparation methods, low cost, and low cytotoxicity [22–29]. There are few studies on the UV absorption characteristics and applications of the CDs [30–33]. Samuel C. Hess et al. [34] synthesized CQDs-PVA films using citric acid and branched polyethyleneimine with high UV-A absorption and maintained more than 80 % light transparency in the visible range. Sung Jun Parka et al. [35] synthesized CDs films using water and acetone, which have strong UV and blue light-absorbing abilities. The optical transparency of all films in the visible region (>500 nm) was maintained above 70 %. However, the prepared CDs film lacks UVB absorption and the transmittance of 40 wt.% CDs film decreases sharply in visible light, resulting in the yellowing of the film. Akshay S. Patil et al. [36] synthesized polyvinyl alcohol/waste tea pomace carbon dot (PVA@WTR-CDs) films using biomass. The PVA@WTR-CDs-3 film successfully blocked all (100 %) UVC and UVB regions, but could only achieve 20–60 % UVA blockage. Increasing the concentration of CDs solution again, 100 % absorption is not attained in the UVA region. In contrast, the transmittance in the visible region decreases, and the film color also shows yellowing problems. Similarly, Xu and his co-workers [37] extracted effective UV harmful 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs from biomass-based materials and functionalized PVA films by combining chitosan (CS) with CQDs to synergistically act as UV absorbers. The PVA/CS/1-CQDs film has blocking capacity of 83.58 % for UVA, and the PVA/CS/2-CQDs film has a UVA blocking ability of up to 94.53 %. But the light transmittance is below 80 % in visible light. In general, carbon dots that achieve full absorption of both UVA and UVB have rarely been reported so far, and maintaining a transmission rate of more than 95 % in the visible region has not been reported up to now. Moreover, only two expensive water-soluble UV absorbers, BP-4 (2-hydroxy-4-methoxy-5-sulfonic acid benzophenone) and UV-T (2-phenyl benzimidazole-5) are currently available on the market [6]. Therefore, developing water-soluble UV absorbers with broad-spectrum absorption properties has received extensive attention. And the prepared polymer films are prone to yellowing, anti-aging, stability and other properties that face enormous challenges [38,39].

Herein, we used a one-step method to synthesize the carbon dots (OP–CDs) based on three-component raw materials and prepared the first-ever broad-spectrum water-soluble UV absorbers with UVB and UVA bands. A powerful and broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots that has not been reported before. Only adding 50 μL (1.7 × 10−2 mg/mL) can achieve the 98 % UVA and 100 % UVB broad-spectrum UV absorption and the 99 % transmittance in visible light. OP–CDs are rich in hydrophilic functional groups and linked to the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) by chemical bonds to prepare the highly transparent (OP–CDs@PVA) film. The characterization results reveal that OP–CDs@PVA film successfully blocked 99 % of the UV radiation (200–400 nm) and maintains high transparency by increasing OP–CDs concentration. Likewise, it shows excellent anti-aging and anti-photodegradation properties, and the incorporation of OP–CDs can improve the inherent mechanical properties of the PVA film.

All chemicals are of analytical grade and do not require further purification during use. o-phenylenediamine (oPD), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), Rhodamine B and fluorescein sodium salt from Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA) and ethylene imine polymer (PEI, Mn ∼ 70000, 50 % aqueous solution) was procured from Aladdin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The OP–CDs were prepared by using citric acid (CA), ethylene imine polymer (PEI) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD). First, 1 g of CA, 0.5 g of PEI and 0.5 g of oPD were very small and wholly dissolved in 20 mL DI water with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then the obtained solution was added to the Teflon-line autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the yellow solution was obtained by filtering the macromolecular particles with a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The solution was then redialed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) with deionized water for 2 h to remove the small molecule particles. Replace DI water at 30 min intervals. Finally, the OP–CDs were obtained by freeze-drying, and made into a 1.0 mg/mL solution for further application without purification.

The OP–CDs@PVA films were fabricated by using a simple solvent casting method [40]. 1.5 g polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dissolved in 30 mL DI water, and stirred at 90 ℃ for 2 h until a transparent solution was obtained. Then the different volumes OP–CDs solutions (0.1 mg, 0.3 mg, 0.9 mg, 1.5 mg, 2.1 mg, 2.7 mg) were added to the PVA solutions, respectively (OP–CDs@PVA-1, OP–CDs@PVA-2, OP–CDs@PVA-3, OP–CDs@PVA-4, OP–CDs@PVA-5, OP–CDs@PVA-6). Subsequently, 6 mL of the mixture solution was dropped into a plastic petri dish with a diameter of 3 cm and dried at 60 ℃ for 2 h to obtain OP–CDs@PVA films (45 ± 3 μm) with different concentrations of OP–CDs.

The UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra (UV-7900, Hitachi) using water or air as a reference from 200 to 800 nm. The Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded by a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectra using KBr as a reference from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku) pattern was measured at the 2θ range from 10° to 80°. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were conducted on a Thermo ScientificTM K-AlphaTM+ spectrometer. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL-2010) images were collected and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with the Hitachi Model F-7000.

CA, oPD and PEI synthesized the OP–CDs via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Fig. 1 illustrates the OP-CD schematic route. The TEM images show that the average size of OP–CDs was 5.57 nm (Fig. 2 a). The HRTEM images exhibit the obvious lattices in carbon cores of OP–CDs (Fig. 2b), which demonstrates the high crystallinity of OP–CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [41]. The XRD pattern of OP–CDs displays a broad peak at 21.7° in Fig. 2d, which can be attributed to the amorphous graphitized structure [42]. Fig. 2e shows functional groups on OP–CDs surface by the FT-IR spectrum. The presented absorption bonds around 3405 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of the CO/CN [43]. The stretching vibrations of NH and CN can be observed at 1454 and 1411 cm−1. The characteristic peak at 1272 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO [44]. The expected peak of the benzene ring comes from 748 cm−1 in the fingerprint region.

The XPS was employed to identify further the surface states and elements of the resultant OP–CDs. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows three typical peaks at 282.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV, respectively, indicating that OP–CDs mainly consist of C (74.95 %, atomic percentage), N (11.88 %, atomic percentage), O (13.17 %, atomic percentage). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2g) reveals three peaks at 283.6, 284.9, and 286.6 eV, ascribed to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO/CN bonds, respectively [45]. Moreover, the spectrum of N 1 s displays two peaks at 397.4 and 399.0 eV (Fig. 2h), which are assigned to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N groups [46]. The spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 2i) indicates the existence of CO and CO/OH groups at 530.0 and 531.2 eV [47]. Overall, combined with XPS and FT-IR analysis, it can be inferred that the surface of OP–CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups such as –NH2, –OH and –COOH, which helps improve the water solubility and dispersibility of OP–CDs.

The Fig. 3 a exhibits the UV–vis absorbance and transmittance spectra of OP–CDs with increasing 30 μL (1.0 × 10−2 mg/mL) to 50 μL (1.7 × 10−2 mg/mL) using water as a reference. Add 30 μL OP–CDs solution to achieve 96 % UVB and 90 % UVA absorption efficiency. By further increasing the volume of OP–CDs solution to 50 μL, that exhibits good absorption effects in both UVA (98 %) and UVB (100 %), and the transmittance of 99 % for visible light. The solution is still colorless and emits bright blue light under the UV lamp (the inset). Thus, a minimal amount of OP–CDs solution can achieve broad-spectrum absorption without changing the solution color, that OP–CDs were expected to become a new type of UV absorbers.

There are mainly-three absorption bands in UV–vis absorbance spectra one strong absorption band at 200–300 nm, and two strong absorption bands at 307 nm and 361 nm from the synergy of CA, PEI and oPD. The absorption band at 200–300 nm is exceptionally high, which results from the fact that the carbon shells of the carbon dots contain multiple cyclic conjugate structures and various functional groups connected by the cyclic structures. When only CDs were synthesized from CA and PEI (Fig. S1b), only a strong absorption band appeared at 350 nm (UVA), which is due to a large number of amide bonds attached to the surface of the carbon core, undergoing an n → π* transition [34]. However, it can’t generate the absorption band of UVB. The oPD is a common nitrogen source in carbon dots synthesis, which tends to form conjugated nitrogen-containing compounds. Fig. S1a shows that oPD helps to increase the π electron groups to cause the π → π* transition of CC bond [35]. In CA and PEI systems, adding oPD will occur adjacent amide and intramolecular carboxyl hydrolysis (Fig. S1c), and lead to the generation of pyrrolic N, so that the XPS will exist the existence of pyrrolic N. With hydrothermal carbonization, increasing the number of pyrrolic N and benzene rings to make the carbon nuclei denser. The system contains more conjugated structures that new absorption bands are created based on the retention of the original absorption bands (Fig. S1d and Fig. S2). As a result, the solid UVB-aborting band (307 nm) is associated with the aromatic sp2 structural region in the OP–CDs. However, as the amount of oPD continued to increase (Fig. S2), the pyrroline N and benzene ring content became more and more, making the absorption intensity of UVB greater than the absorption intensity of UVA, or more robust. Thus, it can also be proved that the absorption band in UVB is attributed to the addition of oPD. The redshift from 350 nm to 361 nm is due to the π-electron delocalization caused by the conjugation effect, which increases the binding force of the orbitals, resulting in lower energy and redshift. Typically, carbon dots have an influential absorption band at 200–250 nm, due to the inherent characteristic peaks caused by the carbon shell of carbon dots containing multiple cyclic conjugate structures and various functional groups connected by the cyclic structures.

The OP–CDs are very famous for their typical optical properties. Therefore, the synthesized OP–CDs were characterized by basic optical properties. Fig. 3b shows PL spectra of OP–CDs. It is pointed out that the maximum intensity of fluorescence at 440 nm was achieved under 400 nm excitation. The synthetic OP–CDs with stable emission peak position show the excitation-independent property, indicating that the fluorescence of OP–CDs originates from surface defects and uniform size. [48] OP–CDs emit bright blue light under a UV lamp (365 nm), which is consistent with the spectrum.

The solution of OP–CDs was maintained at a pH of 0–14 for 48 h for acid and alkaline resistance experiments (Fig. 3(c-d)). The OP–CDs could keep their UV absorption intensity unchanged, indicating that OP–CDs have strong stability under acid or alkaline conditions. However, the commercial aqueous UV absorber BP-4 favors survival under acidic conditions. Under alkaline conditions, the absorption peaks were found to change, possessing only UVA absorption peaks (Fig. S3a). Although UV-T is resistant to acids and bases, it contains only UVB absorption (Fig. S3b). Fig. 3e illustrate the resistance of OP–CDs to high temperature. UV absorption properties of OP–CDs remain unchanged at 60 ℃ for 300 h. After 900 h, although the long-term high-temperature environment caused the evaporation of water and increased the concentration of the solution, its UV absorption properties still maintain good performance. Besides, OP–CDs were placed on the outdoor balcony for 120 days, maintained good UV absorption efficiency (Fig. 3e). For comparison with commercial UV absorbers, UVB (313 nm, 16 W) and UVA (365 nm, 30 W) UV lamps were selected to radiate BP-4 and OP–CDs, respectively (Fig. S4), and the degradation rates were calculated (Table S (2–3)). It is obvious that the degradation rate of OP–CDs is much better than that of BP-4 for both UVA and UVB radiation. As a result, OP-CD provides exceptional stability and anti-UV absorption performance, which contributes to the applicability to practical application environmental conditions.

UV absorbers are usually utilized to prevent the degradation of polymers under UV exposure. In this work, we have used OP–CDs solution as a UV-blocking agent to fabricate OP–CDs@PVA films (45 ± 3 μm). The UV–vis absorption spectra show that increasing OP–CDs enhanced the UV absorption intensity of OP–CDs@PVA using air as reference sample (Fig. 4 a). Pure PVA has almost no UV absorption performance (Fig. S4). When OP–CDs were added to the PVA film, two characteristic absorbent bands appeared in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, in which one is the π → π* transition at 307 nm and the other is the n → π* transition at 361 nm, corresponding to the characteristic UV–vis absorption spectrum of OP–CDs solution, respectively. Possibly OP–CDs could contribute to higher absorption efficiency. The transmittance of PVA and OP–CDs@PVA films are shown in Fig. 4b. When 2.7 mg (0.18 wt%) of OP-CDs were incorporated into the PVA, which can achieve transmittance of more than 99 % in the visible region and UV absorption efficiency is better than 98 % in the UV region. Well below the recommended commercial UV absorber dosage of 3–5 % [6].

UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra can explain qualitative results measured, while the UV digital lux meter helps to measure UV-shielding capability from the polymer film based on quantitative calculation. So, we chose a UV digital lux meter for testing to demonstrate the actual UV-shielding power of the polymer films [18]. First, the daylight as the light source that the pure PVA film without OP–CDs shielded only 3.52 % of UV radiation and OP–CDs@PVA-1 containing blocked 49.78 % under the daylight illumination. As shown in Fig. 4c, OP–CDs@PVA-2 to OP–CDs@PVA-6 UV-shielding abilities are 86.70, 92.56, 96.77 %, 98.49 and 99.40 %, respectively. A similar experiment was performed under daylight for 120 h and the results were consistent as shown in Fig. 4d. After exposure to sunlight for 120 h, the PVA films only shielded 2.84 % of UV light, while other films doped with OP–CDs kept high UV shielding performance. The representative films (OP–CDs@PVA-5 and OP–CDs@PVA-6) could be the optimum percentage for better UV-shielding capability, more than 92.10 % of UV light. Fig. 5 a illustrates the photographs with different contents of OP–CDs@PVA films in the sunlight, that film's color hardly changes and remains transparent with the increase of OP–CDs content. The UV-shielding ability of OP–CDs@PVA film was also observed with the naked eye (Fig. 5b). Rhodamine B can be marked as a rose-red solution, and can fluoresce bright orange through PVA under UV light (Fig. S5). As the concentration of OP–CDs doped in the PVA films increases, the color of Rhodamine B gradually turns red without orange fluorescence. Overall, it is strongly proved that the concentration of OP–CDs plays a vital role in improving the UV-shielding properties of polymer films.

To explore the relationship between fluorescence properties and UV absorption intensity. Compare Fig. 4c and Fig. 6 a, the PL spectra of the pure PVA film and the OP–CDs@PVA films with different doped concentrations of OP–CDs. The pure PVA film has no characteristic fluorescence emission with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the OP–CDs@PVA films gradually increase with the OP–CDs concentration increasing from 0.1 to 2.7 mg, and the luminescence center is located at about 430 nm, which can be used as a light transfer film. The conclusions can be drawn: as the fluorescence intensity of the PVA film doped with different concentrations of OP–CDs gradually increases.

Many economic losses will be caused every year because of the poor anti-aging effect of polymer films. As a result, aging resistance is an essential factor in measuring the goodness of polymer materials [49]. To understand the anti-aging and anti-photodegradation performances of the OP–CDs@PVA films, the aging test and anti-photocatalytic activity of fluorescein sodium salt were carried out respectively. The prepared OP–CDs@PVA-6 film was placed under a UV lamp (30 W) for 120 h. As shown in Fig. 6b, the anti-aging performances of the OP–CDs@PVA-6 film are demonstrated by the transmission spectra. During UV radiation, the composite film maintains its stability and retains nearly 95 % of its UV-shielding performance. Correspondingly, the surface morphology of OP–CDs@PVA-6 film and pure PVA film before and after radiation was studied by SEM, which can be intuitively observed in Fig. S6. OP–CDs@PVA-6 and pure PVA surfaces are exceedingly smooth without UV irradiation (Fig. 6c). After 120 h of UV radiation, the film without the doping of OP–CDs presents a very rough surface with a large area of folds and cracks, which means the destruction of the structure and the severe aging degree of the film were induced by UV light energy. In contrast, the surface of the OP–CDs@PVA-6 film is relatively smooth. It has only slight structural damage, which further confirms the role of the OP–CDs additive in prolonging the service life of PVA against UV radiation.

The photocatalytic activities of OP–CDs were determined by the photocatalytic degradation of fluorescein sodium salt under UV lamp (30 W). Fig. S7 shows the rates of photodegradation resistance regarding fluorescein sodium salt solution (3 mL, 0.2 mg/mL), the fluorescein sodium salt solution coated with OP–CDs@PVA-6 film, and 30 μL OP–CDs solution (3 mL, 0.1 mg/mL) was added to fluorescein sodium salt solution by the UV–vis absorbance spectra, respectively. With the increase of UV radiation, the absorption intensity of pure fluorescein sodium salt solution decreased sharply, and the color gradually became lighter. After 12 h, the absorption intensity of pure fluorescein sodium salt solution decreased to 55 %. Under the same irradiation time, the absorption efficiency of the solution wrapped by the OP–CDs@PVA-6 film and the solution added with OP–CDs maintained 95 % and 92 %, respectively. Compared with the original solution, the color of the solution was almost unchanged, which proved that both OP–CDs@PVA-6 film and fluorescein sodium salt solution doped with OP–CDs solution had the anti-photocatalysis effect. Given the diverse applications of polymer films coating, packaging, and so on, it is vital to study their mechanical properties. Fig. S8a displays the thermal analysis of PVA, OP–CDs@PVA-2, OP–CDs@PVA-4 and OP–CDs@PVA-6. The initial weight loss in all samples (30–120 °C) was due to the evaporation of water. The second stage in the temperature range of 220–360 °C is the melting and decomposition of polyvinyl alcohol [50]. After 380 °C, all samples continue to degrade and an increase in the stability of the OP–CDs film can be seen. In addition, Tg was improved by the introduction of OP–CDs due to the restricted motion of the chain segments (Fig. S8b). We chose three gradient films to assess the mechanical properties (Fig. 6d), which was found that the tensile strength of PVA film was 63.06 MPa, and the tensile strength of OP–CDs@PVA-6 was further increased to 70.96 MPa. The significant enhancement of mechanical properties of OP–CDs@PVA thin films is due to the fact that OP–CDs form a dense network with the PVA matrix through hydrogen bonds in the PVA matrix, and the strong hydrogen bond interaction is conducive to the good dispersion of OP–CDs [37]. The study reveals that the PVA films containing OP–CDs can be used for various applications.

Using a simple bottom-up hydrothermal method, we synthesized highly efficient and broad-spectrum UV absorption carbon dots (OP–CDs). The chemical structure, morphology, and UV-absorbing performance of OP–CDs were investigated. Then the UV absorption principle investigated its acid and alkaline resistance, and stability were discussed. Next, OP–CDs were compounded with PVA, the OP–CDs@PVA films have good transparency. In addition, a synthesized OP–CDs@PVA film could block over 99 % of UV light and maintained more than 99 % transparency for light in the visible range. Even after UV exposure for 120 h, the OP–CDs@PVA film was observed and the film maintained its UV protection efficiency. Moreover, the OP–CDs@PVA film can resist photodegradation and possess mechanical properties. Finally, we studied the relationship between the UV absorption and the fluorescence properties of OP–CDs@PVA, that the UV absorption intensity is positively correlated with the fluorescence intensity. In particular, the UV absorption performance of OP–CDs@PVA film is exceptional, which makes it have potential application prospects in the field of anti-ultraviolet materials.

Jiemin Qiu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Weihao Ye: Methodology, Data curation. Congcong Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Zhiqiang Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Chaofan Hu: Writing – review & editing. Jianle Zhuang: . Hanwu Dong: Supervision. Bingfu Lei: Supervision. Guangqi Hu: Funding acquisition. Yingliang Liu: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12174119 and 52172142). And the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2019A1515110921).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2022.10.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
